questions.question: Define a tree as a data structure and state its essential characteristics that distinguish it from linear data structures.
questions.hint: Focus on hierarchical nature and absence of cycles
keyPoints: Tree is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure
Consists of nodes connected by edges
Has a special node called root
Remaining nodes partitioned into disjoint subtrees
No cycles allowed
Represents parent-child relationships

questions.question: Explain the terms: (i) Root node (ii) Parent node (iii) Child node (iv) Leaf node with respect to tree data structure.
questions.hint: Use a simple tree diagram to illustrate each term
keyPoints: Root: Topmost node with no parent
Parent: Node that has one or more child nodes
Child: Node that has a parent node
Leaf: Node with no children (terminal node)
Each term with a simple example

questions.question: Define and differentiate between the 'depth' and 'height' of a node in a tree with an example.
questions.hint: Remember depth of root = 0, height of leaf = 0
keyPoints: Depth: Number of edges from root to the node (root has depth 0)
Height: Number of edges on the longest path from node to a leaf
Depth is measured from root going downward
Height is measured from node going downward to leaves
Example with calculation for both

questions.question: What is meant by the 'degree' of a node in a tree? Explain with an example and state the degree of leaf nodes.
keyPoints: Degree: Number of children a node has
Leaf nodes have degree 0
Internal nodes have degree >= 1
Example showing nodes with different degrees
Maximum degree of a tree

questions.question: Explain the tree level concept. How is the level of the root node defined? If a node is at level L, at what level are its children?
questions.hint: Most textbooks use root at level 0
keyPoints: Level: Row number in tree hierarchy
Root is at level 0 (or level 1 depending on convention)
Children of level L are at level L+1
Level increases by 1 for each generation
Consistent level numbering throughout tree

questions.question: Define the following terms: (i) Siblings (ii) Ancestors (iii) Descendants (iv) Subtree
questions.hint: Trace paths in a sample tree to identify each
keyPoints: Siblings: Nodes having the same parent
Ancestors: All nodes on path from root to given node
Descendants: All nodes in subtree rooted at given node
Subtree: Tree consisting of a node and all its descendants
Each term with example

questions.question:
Consider the following tree structure:

 A
 / \
 B C
 / \ \
 D E F
 /
 G

Identify and list: (i) Root node (ii) Leaf nodes (iii) Parent of E (iv) Children of B (v) Siblings of D (vi) Ancestors of G (vii) Descendants of B (viii) Degree of B and C (ix) Level of E (x) Height of the tree
questions.hint: Draw the tree and trace each requirement systematically
keyPoints: Root: A
Leaf nodes: D, E, G, F
Parent of E: B
Children of B: D, E
Siblings of D: E
Ancestors of G: B, A
Descendants of B: D, E, G
Degree of B: 2, Degree of C: 1
Level of E: 2
Height of tree: 3

questions.question: Explain the properties of a tree data structure. Derive the relationship between number of nodes and number of edges in a tree.
questions.hint: Use mathematical reasoning to prove N-1 edges
keyPoints: Property 1: One root node
Property 2: Every node except root has exactly one parent
Property 3: No cycles
Property 4: Connected graph
Property 5: N nodes → N-1 edges (for non-empty tree)
Derivation: Each node except root contributes one edge to parent

questions.question: Compare trees with linear data structures (arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues). Discuss why trees are preferred for certain applications.
questions.hint: Focus on hierarchical vs linear organization
keyPoints: Linear: Sequential access, O(n) search
Trees: Hierarchical, faster operations O(log n) in BST
Trees represent natural hierarchical relationships
File systems, organization charts use trees
Efficient insertion and deletion in balanced trees
Moderate time complexity between arrays and linked lists
Real-world modeling capability

questions.question: Explain the differences between a general tree and a binary tree. Can a binary tree be used to represent a general tree? Justify your answer.
questions.hint: Think about transformation techniques between tree types
keyPoints: General tree: No limit on number of children per node
Binary tree: Maximum 2 children per node (left and right)
Binary tree is a specialized form of general tree
Yes, general tree can be converted to binary tree
Conversion method: First child → left child, Next sibling → right child
Applications where each is preferred

questions.question: Draw a tree with at least 8 nodes and label all the following: internal nodes, external nodes, path from root to any leaf, depth of two different nodes, height of two different nodes, and degree of each node.
questions.hint: Create a tree that allows demonstration of all concepts
keyPoints: Tree with minimum 8 nodes drawn
Internal nodes correctly identified (nodes with children)
External nodes (leaves) correctly identified
Path shown from root to a leaf
Depth calculated for at least 2 nodes
Height calculated for at least 2 nodes
Degree shown for each node

questions.question: A tree has 15 nodes. (i) What is the minimum possible height? (ii) What is the maximum possible height? (iii) If the tree is a binary tree, what is the minimum height? (Assume root is at level 0)
questions.hint: Use formula: minimum height = floor(log₂n) for complete tree
keyPoints: (i) Minimum height: Complete binary tree → height = floor(log₂15) = 3
(ii) Maximum height: Linked list structure → height = 14
(iii) Minimum height of binary tree with 15 nodes: 4 (for complete binary tree)
Explanation: Complete binary tree has nodes at all levels except possibly last
Maximum height occurs when each node has only one child

questions.question: In a tree with 'n' nodes, if the sum of degrees of all nodes is 40, find the number of edges in the tree. Also, explain the relationship used.
keyPoints: Relationship: Sum of degrees = 2 × E (where E = number of edges)
This is because each edge connects two nodes
Therefore, 2 × E = 40
Number of edges E = 20
Verification: For tree with n nodes, E = n - 1 = 20 implies n = 21

questions.question: A binary tree has 3 levels. What is the maximum possible number of nodes in this tree? Also, find the minimum number of nodes.
keyPoints: Maximum: Full binary tree at each level
Level 0: 2⁰ = 1 node
Level 1: 2¹ = 2 nodes
Level 2: 2² = 4 nodes
Maximum = 1 + 2 + 4 = 7 nodes
Minimum: Only root at level 0, one child at level 1, one child at level 2
Minimum = 3 nodes

questions.question: What is an 'ordered tree' and an 'unordered tree'? How does ordering affect tree operations?
questions.hint: Think about left child vs right child in binary trees
keyPoints: Ordered tree: Children have specific positions/ordering (left child, right child)
Unordered tree: Children are not in any particular order
Binary tree is an ordered tree (left vs right matters)
Ordering affects comparison, traversal, and insertion operations
Ordered trees have more structure and allow duplicate handling differently
